questions.question: How do grief waves typically change over time?

options.text: They stay the same intensity forever
They disappear completely after the first year
They generally come less frequently and may not last as long, but can still be powerful
They only get worse as more time passes

questions.explanation: Over time, grief waves generally come less frequently and may not last as long. But they can still be powerful, especially when triggered by something meaningful. Waves still coming years later is a sign that the person or experience mattered deeply, not a failure of grieving.

questions.question: What is a grief trigger?

options.text: A sign that your grief is getting worse and you need immediate help
Anything that brings your loss back to the forefront with sudden intensity
A deliberate attempt to force yourself to feel grief
A technique used in grief therapy to confront avoidance

questions.explanation: A trigger is anything that brings your loss back to the forefront with sudden intensity. Triggers can be predictable (anniversaries, holidays) or completely unexpected (a song, a familiar smell, an ordinary moment). They are not setbacks — they are your mind and body remembering what mattered.

questions.question: When a grief wave hits, what is the recommended goal?

options.text: Make the grief stop as quickly as possible
Distract yourself immediately to avoid feeling the pain
Ride the wave — stay present with what you feel without being swept away
Analyze exactly why the wave happened to prevent future ones

questions.explanation: When a wave hits, the goal is not to make it stop. It is to ride it — to stay present with what you are feeling without being swept away. Strategies include grounding, naming emotions, safe expression, reaching out, and reminding yourself that the wave will pass.

questions.question: What are anniversary reactions?

options.text: Celebrations that help you move past grief on meaningful dates
Signs that you have not made any progress in your grief
Temporary increases in grief symptoms around meaningful dates, which are normal even years later
Reactions that only happen on the exact date of the loss

questions.explanation: Anniversary reactions are temporary increases in grief symptoms around meaningful dates. They are extremely common, even years after the loss, and they do not mean you are going backward. They reflect love and memory, not a lack of progress.

questions.question: Why does the lesson recommend naming or labeling emotions during a grief wave?

options.text: It helps you avoid feeling the emotion entirely
It activates the prefrontal cortex, which helps regulate the emotional response
It makes the grief wave last longer so you can process it fully
It is only useful for people who are in therapy

questions.explanation: Simply labeling the emotion can reduce its intensity. For example, saying 'This is a grief wave. I am feeling intense sadness right now. It will pass.' Naming emotions activates the prefrontal cortex, which helps regulate the emotional response.

title: Coping with Grief Waves and Triggers - Quiz
